Address ALL before trusting any backtest.

LIVERMORE'S RULE OF PATIENCE: 'The desire for constant action irrespective of underlying conditions is responsible for many losses in Wall Street.' Crypto's 24/7 market makes this worse—you feel like you should always be in a position. WRONG. The best traders spend 90% of their time waiting. Set alerts. Trade only when your A+ setup appears. Discipline is profitable. Activity is expensive.

THE SYSTEM IS THE EDGE: Individual trade outcomes are random. The system's edge only manifests over hundreds of trades. This means you MUST take every signal—skipping trades breaks the system. In crypto, missing one trade can mean missing the 10x move that makes the year. Define your system. Take EVERY signal. Trust the math over your feelings.

CONTAGION RISK MAP: LTCM's failure cascaded because counterparty risk was hidden. In crypto, map your counterparty exposure: which exchanges hold your assets? Which DeFi protocols? Which stablecoins? If any single counterparty's failure would be catastrophic, diversify immediately. The 2022 contagion (Terra→3AC→Celsius→Voyager→BlockFi→FTX) proved this lesson at massive scale.

DAILY HOMEWORK: Every day: 1) Review open positions. 2) Check stops and targets. 3) Scan for new setups. 4) Update trading journal. 5) Review market conditions. This 30-minute ritual is non-negotiable. In crypto, add: check on-chain metrics (exchange flows, whale movements), funding rates, and macro calendar. Homework prevents surprises and maintains discipline.

LOSSES ARE THE COST OF BUSINESS: Every business has costs—rent, salaries, supplies. For traders, losses are the cost of doing business. Would you stop paying rent because you had a slow month? No. Don't stop taking trades because of recent losses. The next trade could be the big winner that pays for all the small losses. If you skip it due to fear, you break the system.

THETA DECAY ACCELERATION: Time decay (theta) accelerates exponentially in the final 30 days before option expiry. If short options, this benefits you—premium evaporates faster. If long options, this hurts you—your position bleeds daily. In crypto, use 30-60 DTE options for long positions (slower decay). Sell weekly/bi-weekly options to collect accelerated theta. Manage gamma risk near expiry.

GREED IS CONTAGIOUS: "Everyone has their price" for corruption—and in manias, the market price of greed is extremely low. When your Uber driver, barber, and grandmother all ask about crypto, the marginal buyer pool is nearly exhausted. New entrants have the least knowledge and highest leverage. Their capitulation drives the crash. Sell when your taxi driver gives you crypto tips.

LIVERMORE'S RECORD-KEEPING: He kept detailed ledgers of every trade, every pattern observed, every lesson learned. He reviewed these records constantly. This made him his own teacher. In crypto, maintain a trade journal with: entry/exit prices, setup type, market conditions, emotional state, and post-trade analysis. Review weekly. The patterns in YOUR behavior are the most profitable patterns to discover.

EMA CONFLUENCE ZONES: When multiple EMAs (8, 21, 50, 200) converge tightly, a major move is imminent. Direction is unknown until breakout. In crypto, EMA compression on daily chart preceded every major BTC move of 30%+ in the last 5 years. Trade the breakout with momentum confirmation. Stop below the EMA cluster. Target = previous range projected from breakout.

END-OF-DAY POSITION REVIEW: At session close (4PM EST traditional, 00:00 UTC crypto), review all open positions against your plan. Are stops still appropriate? Has the daily thesis changed? In crypto, the Asian session (00:00-08:00 UTC) often reverses the US session direction. Consider reducing position size before the handoff if you won't be monitoring.

OPENING HOUR DYNAMICS: Livermore observed that the first hour of trading reveals the day's character—gap direction, opening volume, and initial price action set the tone. In crypto, the 08:00-10:00 UTC window (European open) and 13:30-15:30 UTC (US equity open) are the highest-volume periods. Breakouts during these windows have higher success rates than random-time breakouts.

OPENING DRIVE ANALYSIS: First 30 minutes of major session opens show institutional intent. In crypto, watch for large market orders at 08:00 UTC (EU open) and 13:30 UTC (US futures open). If the opening drive is absorbed (price reverses), it's a trap. If it accelerates with increasing volume, the trend is genuine. Use delta (buy vs sell volume) to measure absorption vs continuation.

WYCKOFF SPRING PATTERN: Price briefly dips below support (the 'spring') then rapidly reverses upward, trapping shorts below the level. Volume surges on the recovery. In crypto, Wyckoff springs at key weekly support levels (e.g., 200-EMA) are among the highest-probability long entries. Confirm with: 1) Spring below support for <4 hours. 2) Volume spike on recovery. 3) Close back above support.

WYCKOFF ACCUMULATION SCHEMATIC: Phase A (selling climax → automatic rally → secondary test). Phase B (building cause—range-bound). Phase C (spring—final shakeout below support). Phase D (sign of strength—markup begins). Phase E (markup—trend resumes). In crypto, this multi-week accumulation pattern precedes every major BTC bull run. Identify Phase C (spring) for optimal entry.

DELTA DIVERGENCE SIGNAL: When price makes new highs but delta (buy volume minus sell volume) makes lower highs = sellers are absorbing the rally—distribution is occurring. When price makes new lows but delta makes higher lows = buyers are absorbing the decline—accumulation. In crypto, delta divergence on 4H/daily charts marks major turning points 60-70% of the time.

WYCKOFF SPRING AS TURTLE FAILURE: The Turtle system's 20-day low breakout entry gets stopped out by Wyckoff springs—the false breakdown that shakes out trend followers before the real move begins. After being stopped on a spring, re-enter if: 1) Price recovers above the breakdown level within 3 bars. 2) Volume increases on recovery. This recaptures the false-break trade.

ORDER FLOW ANALYSIS FOR CRYPTO: Monitor the order book for: 1) Large resting orders (walls) that get pulled (spoofing). 2) Aggressive market orders (taker buy/sell ratio). 3) Delta (net buying vs selling pressure). In crypto, order book data is freely available on most exchanges. Track the bid-ask imbalance—if takers are aggressively buying, price is likely to move up short-term.

WYCKOFF SPRING AS REFLEXIVE BOTTOM: Soros's reflexivity explains why Wyckoff springs mark bottoms—the panic selling that creates the spring is the final capitulation of weak hands. Once exhausted, the reflexive loop reverses: rising prices attract buyers, which raises prices further. The spring IS the moment where the far-from-equilibrium downward trend exhausts itself.

DELTA DIVERGENCE STATISTICAL VALIDATION: Academic studies on order flow show that cumulative delta divergence from price predicts reversals with 55-65% accuracy across multiple markets. In crypto, the signal is stronger due to less sophisticated participants and more behavioral trading. Apply statistical significance testing—require at least 30 instances before trusting the pattern.

CRYPTO ORDER BOOK READING: On exchanges like Binance, the order book shows all limit orders at each price level. Key signals: 1) Spoofing—large orders placed then removed to manipulate direction. 2) Stacking—multiple orders at the same level for genuine support/resistance. 3) Iceberg orders—hidden large orders that only show small portions. Watch for order book imbalance as short-term direction signal.

CLOSING PRICE SIGNIFICANCE: Livermore considered the close the most important price of the day—it represents the final verdict of all participants. In crypto, the daily close (00:00 UTC) is the most important candle for technical analysis. Weekly close (Sunday 00:00 UTC) is the most significant for swing trading. A strong close near the high/low of the day signals continuation.

PRE-MARKET PREPARATION: Before market open, review: 1) Overnight price action. 2) Key levels from previous day. 3) Any overnight news/events. 4) Pre-set orders at planned entry/exit levels. In crypto's 24/7 market, define your 'session' (e.g., your waking hours). Before starting your trading session, check what happened while you slept. Set alerts for key levels so you don't miss overnight moves.

SHAKEOUT BEFORE BREAKOUT: The Turtles' system often got whipsawed by 'shakeout' moves—false breakdowns that triggered stops before the real trend began. This is exactly the Wyckoff spring. The solution: use a 're-entry' rule—if stopped out and price reclaims the level within 3 bars, re-enter the original position. This recaptures the trend after the shakeout.

VOLUME-PRICE DIVERGENCE SCIENTIFIC BASIS: Aronson's research confirms that volume-price divergences have statistical validity across multiple markets. Rising price + declining volume = distribution. Falling price + declining volume = accumulation. In crypto, these signals are even more pronounced because volume is the primary honest indicator (harder to fake than price). Require statistical significance before trading.

OPTIONS ORDER FLOW AS SIGNAL: Unusual options activity (large block trades, volume spikes in specific strikes) reveals institutional positioning. In crypto (Deribit), track: 1) Put/Call ratio extremes. 2) Large block trades in specific strikes. 3) Implied volatility changes across the surface. Unusual put buying before a crash, or call buying before a rally, is a leading signal.

LIQUIDITY EXPANSION ENTRY: Enter trend-following positions when liquidity expands—volume surges, bid-ask spreads narrow, and volatility increases. In crypto, liquidity expansion often accompanies breakout moves. The opposite—liquidity contraction (declining volume, widening spreads)—signals range-bound conditions. Match strategy to liquidity regime: trend-follow during expansion, mean-revert during contraction.

LOW VOLUME CONSOLIDATION: Livermore noted that stocks often consolidated on low volume before making big moves. In crypto, when BTC volume drops below 50% of its 20-day average for 5+ days, a volatility expansion is imminent. Direction is unknown. Prepare for the move with breakout orders in both directions. The longer the consolidation, the bigger the eventual move.

END-OF-WEEK POSITION MANAGEMENT: Reduce position sizes by 50% before the weekend. Crypto weekend liquidity is lower, making price more susceptible to manipulation and gap moves. CME futures close Friday and reopen Sunday—BTC can gap significantly. Weekend moves are often reversed Monday. If you hold over weekend, reduce risk and widen stops slightly.

VOLUME CONFIRMS TREND: In a genuine uptrend, volume should increase on rallies and decrease on pullbacks. In a genuine downtrend, volume increases on declines and decreases on bounces. If volume contradicts price (price rising but volume declining), the trend is losing momentum. In crypto, volume analysis on BTC daily chart is the single most reliable trend confirmation tool.

LIQUIDITY AND MARKET EFFICIENCY: The random walk hypothesis holds better in liquid markets where information is quickly priced in. Crypto markets are less liquid than equities, creating more exploitable inefficiencies during low-liquidity periods. However, these inefficiencies come with higher execution costs (slippage). Net edge = gross edge minus execution costs. Trade the most liquid instruments.

LIQUIDITY VACUUM EVENTS: When everyone tries to exit simultaneously, liquidity evaporates. In 2008, mortgage-backed securities had zero bids. In crypto, during crashes, order books thin dramatically—spreads widen 10x, slippage exceeds 5%. This is when stop-losses execute 10-20% from trigger price. Mitigate with: limit orders (not market), smaller position sizes, and multiple exchange access.

LIQUIDITY IS NOT GUARANTEED: Most trading models assume you can exit at or near your desired price. In crypto, liquidity can vanish in seconds during crashes. Your 'stop-loss' at $50K might fill at $45K during a flash crash. Account for this in position sizing—assume worst-case slippage of 5-10% on stops during high-vol events. If that makes the trade unprofitable on paper, don't take it.

VOLUME BREAKOUT VALIDATION: A breakout on volume 2x+ the 20-day average is likely genuine. A breakout on below-average volume is likely a trap. In crypto, monitor 24h volume relative to market cap—high volume/market-cap ratio during breakouts indicates broad participation. Low ratio = likely manipulation or low-conviction move. Wait for volume confirmation before entering breakout trades.

LIQUIDATION-DRIVEN BEAR MARKETS: Shiller's bubble model implies that when the marginal buyer is exhausted, forced selling (liquidations) drives prices far below fundamental value. In crypto, DeFi protocol liquidations, exchange margin calls, and leveraged position liquidations create cascading forced selling. Monitor aggregate liquidation levels—when $1B+ in liquidations cluster at a price, expect violent moves through that level.

REFLEXIVE LIQUIDITY: In Soros's framework, rising prices attract more capital (liquidity expansion), which drives prices further. Falling prices repel capital (liquidity contraction), which drives prices lower. This reflexive liquidity cycle is especially pronounced in crypto where capital flows between DeFi, CEX, and stablecoins are fast and frictionless. Trade with expanding liquidity, reduce when it contracts.

BANK RUN PSYCHOLOGY IN CRYPTO: The same psychology that drives bank runs drives exchange outflows during crypto crises. If depositors believe others are withdrawing, rational behavior is to withdraw too—even if the exchange is solvent. This self-fulfilling prophecy can kill solvent exchanges (FTX). Diversify across exchanges. Keep only active trading capital on any single platform.

CRYPTO MARKET HOURS: While crypto trades 24/7, distinct sessions exist. Asian session (00:00-08:00 UTC): historically bearish bias for BTC. European session (08:00-16:00 UTC): higher volatility, more institutional flow. US session (13:30-21:00 UTC): highest volume, most directional moves. Weekend sessions: lower volume, more susceptible to manipulation. Trade during highest-volume sessions for best execution.

LIQUIDITY-BASED POSITION SIZING: Reduce position size when liquidity contracts (declining volume, widening spreads). In crypto, when BTC daily volume drops below 30-day average by >50%, reduce position sizes by 50%. Low liquidity means: 1) Harder to exit. 2) Larger slippage. 3) More susceptibility to manipulation. Liquidity is a risk factor—price it into your position sizing.

POST-CLOSE JOURNALING: After your trading session, document: 1) What you planned vs what you executed. 2) Emotional state during trades. 3) Any plan violations and why. 4) Market observations. 5) Tomorrow's key levels and scenarios. This 15-minute ritual is the most valuable trading activity. The journal becomes your edge—showing you patterns in YOUR behavior that you can exploit or correct.

MORNING GAP ANALYSIS: A gap up at session open with expanding volume = bullish signal (buyers overwhelmed sellers overnight). Gap down with expanding volume = bearish. In crypto, CME futures gaps (weekend gaps) fill ~70% of the time but not immediately. Intraday gaps at session opens (EU/US) with volume are more actionable—trade in direction of gap with stop inside gap range.

OPTIONS EXPIRY DAY MANAGEMENT: On Deribit expiry day (Friday 08:00 UTC), close or roll positions by Thursday. Expiry day is chaotic—pin risk, gamma squeezes, and erratic price action as market makers adjust hedges. In crypto, monthly/quarterly expiries (last Friday of month) are especially significant. Post-expiry, the removal of hedging flows often causes directional moves.

TURTLE SYSTEM ENTRY TIMING: The Turtles entered on new 20-day highs/lows regardless of time of day. However, breakouts during high-volume sessions (US open) were more reliable than breakouts during Asian quiet hours. Adapt for crypto: weight breakouts occurring during 13:30-21:00 UTC (US session) more heavily than overnight breakouts. Volume-confirmed breakouts at session open are strongest.

TIME-OF-DAY EFFECTS IN CRYPTO: Statistical analysis shows BTC returns are not uniform across hours. US session (13:30-21:00 UTC) generates disproportionate returns. Asian session has a slight negative bias. These patterns persist across years and are statistically significant. Incorporate time-of-day as a factor in entry/exit timing—enter during high-return periods, avoid low-return periods.

CONTAGION PLANNING: Before entering any crypto position, answer: 'What happens if the largest exchange collapses? If the largest stablecoin depegs? If BTC drops 50% overnight?' If any scenario leads to account ruin, reduce position size. The 2022 contagion chain (Terra→3AC→Celsius→FTX) destroyed fortunes of traders who assumed 'it can't all happen at once.' It can. It did.

RESULTS-ORIENTED THINKING: Don't evaluate your trading by today's P&L. Evaluate by the quality of your decisions. A day where you followed your plan and lost money is a GOOD day. A day where you broke your plan and made money is a BAD day (it reinforces bad behavior). At session close, grade your PROCESS (A/B/C), not your outcome. Process improvement leads to outcome improvement.

FALSE BREAKOUT RECOVERY ENTRY: When a trend-following breakout fails (false breakdown/spring), the recovery is often explosive. Strategy: 1) Get stopped on initial breakout. 2) Watch for recovery above the breakdown level. 3) Re-enter on recovery with stop below the false breakdown low. 4) The false move was the stop-hunt—the real move follows. This is Wyckoff spring logic applied to trend following.

EXCHANGE SELECTION FOR LIQUIDITY: During high volatility, not all exchanges have equal liquidity. Binance > Coinbase > FTX (dead) > smaller exchanges. For large orders, check order book depth across exchanges. Use exchange aggregators (1inch for DEX, or Coinglass for futures) to find best execution. In crisis, some exchanges halt withdrawals—have accounts on multiple exchanges for redundancy.

CLIMAX VOLUME: Livermore recognized that extreme volume at trend extremes signaled exhaustion—buying climax (high volume at top) or selling climax (high volume at bottom). In crypto, daily volume >3x the 20-day average at a price extreme = likely climax. Don't chase climax moves—wait for pullback. Climax volume + bearish candle pattern = high-probability reversal.

WEEKLY CLOSE SIGNIFICANCE: Shiller emphasizes that market narratives crystallize around weekly timeframes. The weekly candle close is the most significant for crypto cycle analysis. A weekly close above key resistance (e.g., BTC above $50K) shifts narratives. A weekly close below support triggers bearish narrative shifts. Use weekly close for strategic decisions, daily for tactical entries.

SOROS'S FIRST-MOVER ADVANTAGE: Soros positioned early, before the crowd recognized the trend. In crypto, first-mover advantage means entering breakouts at session opens before confirmation by the broader market. If BTC breaks above resistance during the Asian session (low participation), the European and US sessions will amplify the move. Enter early, add on confirmation.

DAILY CANDLE ANALYSIS: At session close, analyze the daily candle: 1) Long body with small wicks = strong trend conviction. 2) Doji/small body = indecision, potential reversal. 3) Long upper wick = selling pressure (bearish). 4) Long lower wick = buying pressure (bullish). In crypto, daily candles are the most reliable timeframe for candlestick pattern analysis. Weekly candles for longer-term positioning.

LIQUIDITY PREMIUM: LTCM's models didn't account for liquidity risk—they assumed they could always exit at fair value. In crypto, liquidity risk is the #1 risk after directional risk. Illiquid positions deserve a 'liquidity discount'—don't pay the same price for an illiquid altcoin as for BTC. If you can't exit in <1 hour without moving the market >2%, the position is too large.

PORTFOLIO REBALANCING CADENCE: Malkiel advocates regular rebalancing to maintain target allocations. In crypto, rebalance monthly or when any position deviates >20% from target weight. This forces you to sell winners and buy losers—a systematic contrarian strategy. In bull markets, rebalancing reduces returns slightly but in bear markets it dramatically reduces drawdowns.

RANGE-BOUND TRADING IN LOW LIQUIDITY: When liquidity contracts and Bollinger Bands narrow, switch from trend-following to mean-reversion. Buy at range support, sell at range resistance. In crypto, this is effective during consolidation phases between major trends. Use RSI oversold/overbought for entries. Exit if range breaks (close outside Bollinger Band with volume).

NEWS REACTION ANALYSIS: How a market reacts to news reveals its true condition. Good news + price up = healthy market. Good news + price down = distribution (smart money selling into strength). Bad news + price up = accumulation (smart money buying the dip). Bad news + price down = genuine weakness. In crypto, monitor reaction to halving events, ETF news, and regulatory announcements.

GAMMA SQUEEZE AT SPRING BOTTOMS: When Wyckoff spring occurs near options max-pain, market makers who are short puts must delta-hedge by buying the underlying. This gamma squeeze amplifies the recovery move. In crypto (Deribit), track max-pain price. If a spring occurs near max-pain during high open interest, the recovery will be explosive due to forced delta-hedging buying.

HALVING ANTICIPATION CYCLE: The crypto market prices in halving events 6-12 months in advance. Track halving proximity: 12+ months before = accumulation phase. 6-12 months = early markup. 0-6 months = anticipation peak. Post-halving = 'buy the rumor, sell the news' potential. In the weeks before halving, watch for increasing spot demand and declining exchange reserves.

MULTI-TIMEFRAME CONFIRMATION: Livermore used daily, weekly, and monthly data to confirm his positions. In crypto: monthly chart for cycle direction, weekly for trend direction, daily for entry timing, 4H for precision entry. A trade that aligns across all four timeframes is highest conviction. A conflict between timeframes = reduce size or sit out. The daily close is the most important data point.

REGIME-DEPENDENT STRATEGY SELECTION: Different strategies work in different regimes. In trending markets, momentum strategies outperform. In ranging markets, mean-reversion strategies outperform. In high-volatility markets, volatility-selling strategies work. The key: IDENTIFY THE REGIME FIRST, then select the appropriate strategy. Use ADX for trending/ranging, VIX-equivalent (BVOL) for vol regime.

RECOVERY FROM DRAWDOWNS: A 10% loss requires 11% gain to recover. A 50% loss requires 100% gain. A 90% loss requires 900% gain. This asymmetry means large drawdowns are exponentially harder to recover from. In crypto's volatility, a single bad trade with no stop can set you back years. Protect capital above all else. The math of drawdown recovery is unforgiving.

FED POLICY AND CRYPTO LIQUIDITY: Fed tightening (rate hikes, QT) reduces global dollar liquidity, which flows out of risk assets including crypto. Fed easing (rate cuts, QE) increases dollar liquidity flowing into crypto. The correlation between Fed balance sheet and BTC price is >0.7 over multi-year periods. Trade crypto with the macro tide—don't fight the Fed.

TRAILING STOP METHODS: 1) ATR trailing stop (2-3x ATR below highest close). 2) Moving average trail (close below 20-EMA). 3) Parabolic SAR. 4) Chandelier exit. In crypto, ATR trailing works best during volatile trends. Moving average trails work best during smooth trends. Select method based on current volatility regime. Never use fixed-percentage trails—they're too rigid for crypto's varying volatility.

SYSTEM COMPLETENESS: A complete trading system must define: 1) Market universe. 2) Entry rules. 3) Exit rules. 4) Position sizing. 5) Risk management. 6) Portfolio limits. If any element is missing, it's not a system—it's a collection of ideas. In crypto, many traders have entry rules but no exit rules, or sizing but no risk limits. Build the COMPLETE system before trading real money.

SCANNING FOR TRADES: Use screeners to find setups rather than manually charting hundreds of coins. Filter by: 1) Trend direction (above 200-EMA). 2) Volume surge (>2x average). 3) RSI between 30-70 (not extreme). 4) Proximity to support/resistance. In crypto, TradingView screener or CoinGecko filters work. Scan during your pre-market routine. Only trade the top 3-5 setups.

LOW VOLATILITY REGIME OPTIONS STRATEGIES: When crypto IV is below 50% annualized (rare but happens during extended consolidation), buy straddles/strangles cheaply. The 'volatility risk premium' is minimal during these periods. If a breakout is imminent (BB squeeze, triangle formation), a long straddle captures the move regardless of direction. Close when IV doubles from entry level.

INFORMATION INCORPORATION SPEED: The random walk hypothesis says all available information is already in price. In crypto, information is incorporated faster in BTC (seconds) than in altcoins (minutes to hours). This means: 1) BTC leads. 2) Altcoins lag. 3) Trade altcoins based on BTC's reaction to news. If BTC sells off on FOMC news, sell alts immediately—they'll follow with a delay.

LIVERMORE ON CROWD FOLLOWING: 'The public always wants to be told. The ticker is the great leveler.' He meant that the tape tells the truth even when people lie. In crypto, ignore the narratives on Twitter/Discord. Look at what participants are DOING (order flow, funding rates, on-chain movements), not what they're SAYING. Actions > words. Always.

NARRATIVE SHIFT TIMING: Major narrative shifts in crypto coincide with external catalysts: 1) Halving events (supply shock narrative). 2) ETF approvals (institutional adoption narrative). 3) Macro crises (safe haven narrative). 4) Protocol upgrades (technology narrative). Track when the dominant narrative changes—each new narrative attracts a new wave of marginal buyers and extends the cycle.

ENTRY CHECKLIST: Before EVERY trade: 1) Weekly trend direction identified? 2) Daily trend aligned? 3) Key level identified? 4) Entry trigger present? 5) Stop level defined? 6) Risk/reward >2:1? 7) Position size within 2% risk? 8) Correlation check with existing positions? If any answer is NO, skip the trade. This 60-second checklist prevents 80% of bad trades.

INSTITUTIONAL ARROGANCE: LTCM's Nobel laureates believed their models were superior because of their credentials. In crypto, similar arrogance comes from early success. 'I turned $1K into $100K—I must be a genius.' No, you were lucky in a bull market. Humility is the antidote. Assume every trade could be your last. Treat each position as if your career depends on it. Because it does.

THE LOSING STREAK MENTALITY: After 5+ consecutive losses, most traders abandon their system. This is exactly when the next trade is most likely to be the big winner—if the system is valid. The solution: 1) Trade small enough that a 10-trade losing streak doesn't emotionally devastate you. 2) Track your system's maximum expected drawdown from backtesting. 3) If current drawdown exceeds historical max, THEN review the system.
